section.title: Give Thanks, for He Is Good
section.sectionRationale: The opening psalm of Book V, Psalms 107 to 150, which answers the exile and gathers the redeemed community into a fresh season of praise. Its opening line, Give thanks to the LORD, for He is good, His loving devotion endures forever, deliberately picks up the refrain that closed Psalm 106 and Book IV, and its call for the redeemed of the LORD to say so, verse 2, invites the whole congregation, gathered from every direction, to tell their own stories of rescue.
section.thesis: The LORD redeems His people out of four representative distresses, wandering, imprisonment, sickness, and the storm at sea, always in answer to a desperate cry, so that every rescued soul, whatever the shape of the trouble, owes Him the same public thanksgiving and the same wise consideration of His steadfast love.
section.frameNote: Seven movements: give thanks, let the redeemed say so, verses 1 to 3; wanderers gathered and fed, verses 4 to 9; prisoners set free, verses 10 to 16; fools healed from the brink of death, verses 17 to 22; sailors delivered from the storm, verses 23 to 32; the LORD who reverses fortunes, verses 33 to 38; and a closing judgment, mercy, and call to wisdom, verses 39 to 43.

units.label: Let the redeemed of the LORD say so
units.note: give thanks, let the redeemed say so

verses.text: Give thanks to the LORD, for He is good; His loving devotion endures forever.
Let the {{gaal|redeemed}} of the LORD say so, whom He has redeemed from the hand of the enemy
and gathered from the lands, from east and west, from north and south.

units.label: He led them on a straight path

verses.text: Some wandered in desert wastelands, finding no path to a city in which to dwell.
They were hungry and thirsty; their soul fainted within them.
Then they cried out to the LORD in their trouble, and He delivered them from their distress.
He led them on a straight path to reach a city where they could live.
Let them give thanks to the LORD for His loving devotion and His wonders to the sons of men.
For He satisfies the thirsty and fills the hungry with good things.
Some sat in darkness and in the shadow of death, prisoners in affliction and chains,
because they rebelled against the words of God and despised the counsel of the Most High.
He humbled their hearts with hard labor; they stumbled, and there was no one to help.
Then they cried out to the LORD in their trouble, and He saved them from their distress.
He brought them out of darkness and the shadow of death and broke away their chains.
Let them give thanks to the LORD for His loving devotion and His wonders to the sons of men.
For He has broken down the gates of bronze and cut through the bars of iron.

units.label: He sent forth His word and healed them
units.note: fools healed from the brink of death

verses.text: Fools, in their rebellious ways, and through their iniquities, suffered affliction.
They loathed all food and drew near to the gates of death.
Then they cried out to the LORD in their trouble, and He saved them from their distress.
He sent forth His word and healed them; He rescued them from the Pit.
Let them give thanks to the LORD for His loving devotion and His wonders to the sons of men.
Let them offer sacrifices of thanksgiving and declare His works with rejoicing.

units.label: He calmed the storm to a whisper

verses.text: Others went out to sea in ships, conducting trade on the mighty waters.
They saw the works of the LORD, and His wonders in the deep.
For He spoke and raised a tempest that lifted the waves of the sea.
They mounted up to the heavens, then sunk to the depths; their courage melted in their anguish.
They reeled and staggered like drunkards, and all their skill was useless.
Then they cried out to the LORD in their trouble, and He brought them out of their distress.
He {{damam|calmed}} the storm to a whisper, and the waves of the sea were hushed.
They rejoiced in the silence, and He guided them to the harbor they desired.
Let them give thanks to the LORD for His loving devotion and His wonders to the sons of men.
Let them exalt Him in the assembly of the people and praise Him in the council of the elders.

units.label: He turns a desert into pools of water

verses.text: He turns rivers into deserts, springs of water into thirsty ground,
and fruitful land into fields of salt, because of the wickedness of its dwellers.
He turns a desert into pools of water and a dry land into flowing springs.
He causes the hungry to settle there, that they may establish a city in which to dwell.
They sow fields and plant vineyards that yield a fruitful harvest.
He blesses them, and they multiply greatly; He does not let their herds diminish.

units.label: Let him who is wise pay heed
units.note: judgment, mercy, and a closing call to wisdom

verses.text: When they are decreased and humbled by oppression, evil, and sorrow,
He pours out contempt on the nobles and makes them wander in a trackless wasteland.
But He lifts the needy from affliction and increases their families like flocks.
The upright see and rejoice, and all iniquity shuts its mouth.
Let him who is wise pay heed to these things and consider the loving devotion of the LORD.

gaal.gloss: To redeem, to act as a kinsman-redeemer who buys back or rescues at cost. The psalm's opening call, let the redeemed say so, verse 2, invites every rescued person to publicly testify to this costly deliverance.

damam.gloss: To be silent, still, hushed. God calms the raging storm to this stillness, verse 29, the same divine authority Jesus exercises when He rebukes the wind and sea and says, Peace, be still, Mark 4:39.

basic.context: Opening Book V of the Psalter, this psalm gathers the voices of the redeemed, likely returning exiles among them, verse 3, and invites them to tell their own rescue stories under four pictures: wanderers lost and fed, prisoners freed, the sick healed, and sailors saved from the storm. Each scene follows the same pattern, crisis, cry, and rescue, ending in a call to give thanks.
basic.hermeneutics: The four scenes function as representative categories into which any sufferer's story can be read, not merely as four unrelated historical anecdotes; the repeated refrain, let them give thanks, makes this an invitation for the reader's own testimony, not just a report of what God once did for others.
basic.originalLanguages: Those saved are the geulim, the gaal, redeemed, verse 2, those bought back at cost from the enemy's hand. The storm's fury is calmed to damam, stillness, verse 29, the very word and divine authority Jesus later claims for Himself over the sea.
basic.typology: The sailors' cry answered by a calmed sea, verses 23-30, directly anticipates Jesus calming the storm on the Sea of Galilee, Mark 4:35-41, where the disciples ask the very question this psalm answers: who is this, that even the wind and the sea obey Him.
basic.christ: Jesus' calming of the storm, Mark 4:35-41, deliberately echoes this psalm's fourth scene, He commands the wind and waves with the same word, be still, damam, that this psalm attributes to the LORD alone, verse 29, revealing His own divine identity to disciples who had sung this psalm all their lives.
basic.god: God is shown hearing every kind of cry, from wanderers lost in the wilderness to prisoners in chains to the sick at death's door to sailors terrified at sea, and answering each with the same undeserved rescue, all traced back to His enduring loving devotion, the psalm's opening and closing note.
basic.crossReferences: Mark 4:35-41 shows Jesus calming the storm with the same authority this psalm ascribes to God. Luke 4:18 shows Jesus proclaiming liberty to captives, echoing the psalm's prisoners set free. Isaiah 43:5-7 promises the gathering from every direction that this psalm celebrates as already underway.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's fourfold testimony of rescue, wanderers fed, prisoners freed, the sick healed, sailors saved, is the pattern that every believer's own redemption follows, and it climaxes in Christ, who redeems from the ultimate distress of sin and death and who commands the very storms this psalm describes.

context.literary: A thanksgiving psalm structured as four parallel scenes, each following an identical fourfold pattern: crisis, verses 4, 10, 17, 23; cry, verses 6, 13, 19, 28; rescue, verses 7, 14, 20, 29; and a call to thanks, verses 8, 15, 21, 31, followed by a closing meditation on God's reversing power, verses 33 to 43.
context.historical: Opens Book V, Psalms 107 to 150, and its language of gathering the redeemed from east, west, north, and south, verse 3, fits the post-exilic return from Babylon, giving fresh voice to a people who had literally walked desert roads home.
context.canonical: Its opening line deliberately repeats the refrain that closes Psalm 106 and Book IV, Give thanks to the LORD, for He is good, His loving devotion endures forever, binding the two books together and turning the plea to be gathered, Psalm 106:47, into this psalm's answered praise, gathered from the lands, verse 3.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To gather the testimonies of God's redeemed people, whatever the specific shape of their distress, into a single pattern of crisis, cry, and rescue, and to press every hearer toward both public thanksgiving, let the redeemed say so, and thoughtful wisdom, verse 43.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes particular historical rescues, likely including the return from exile; it prescribes as an abiding pattern crying out to God in any distress and responding to rescue with public testimony and considered wisdom rather than quiet, private relief alone.

debates.question: Are the four distresses, wandering, imprisonment, sickness, and the storm, meant as literal historical case studies or as representative pictures covering every kind of trouble?

views.label: Each scene reflects a genuine, distinct historical experience
views.case: The details are concrete and plausible: returning exiles crossing real deserts, prisoners in real chains, the genuinely sick, and merchants who really sailed the Mediterranean; nothing requires reading them as anything other than real accounts.

views.label: The four scenes are deliberately stylized to be representative
views.case: The identical refrain repeated after each scene, and the round number of four categories covering land, captivity, body, and sea, suggests the psalmist designed these as representative types so that every reader's own trouble could be read into one of them.

debates.bearing: The two readings are not in tension: real historical distress, likely including the exile's hardships, is deliberately universalized by the psalm's structure and refrain so that every generation's redeemed, including believers rescued from sin through Christ, can say, let the redeemed of the LORD say so.

study.originalLanguages: Those saved are the gaal, redeemed, verse 2, bought back at cost from the enemy's hand. The storm's fury is calmed to damam, hushed stillness, verse 29, the very word and divine authority Jesus later claims for Himself over the sea, Mark 4:39.

typology.type: The sailors' cry answered by a calmed sea, verses 23-30
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Jesus calming the storm on the Sea of Galilee, Mark 4:35-41, where the disciples ask exactly what this psalm has already answered, who commands the wind and the waves.

typology.type: The redeemed gathered from every direction to give thanks, verses 2-3
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Christ's gathering of the redeemed from every nation into one people, Revelation 7:9-10, and His shepherding of scattered sheep into one flock, John 10:16.

study.christ: Jesus' calming of the storm, Mark 4:35-41, deliberately echoes this psalm's fourth scene: He commands the wind and waves with the same divine authority, be still, this psalm attributes to the LORD alone, verse 29, revealing His own identity to disciples who would have known this psalm well. The psalm's opening call, let the redeemed of the LORD say so, verse 2, is the pattern of every Christian testimony of rescue from sin through Christ.
study.god: God hears every kind of cry, from wanderers lost in the wilderness to prisoners in chains, from the sick at death's door to sailors terrified at sea, and answers each with the same undeserved rescue, always traced back to His loving devotion that endures forever, the note that opens and closes the psalm.

crossReferences.note: Jesus calms the storm with the same divine authority this psalm ascribes to God alone in verse 29.
Jesus proclaims liberty to captives, echoing this psalm's picture of prisoners broken free from their chains, verses 10-16.
Promises the gathering of God's people from every direction that this psalm celebrates as underway, verse 3.
The confession of the redeemed, let the redeemed say so, verse 2, finds its New Testament shape in the public confession of salvation through Christ.

study.oneStory: This psalm's fourfold testimony of rescue, wanderers fed, prisoners freed, the sick healed, sailors saved, is the pattern that every believer's own redemption follows, and it climaxes in Christ, who redeems from the ultimate distress of sin and death and who commands the very storms this psalm describes.

sermon.title: Considering the Great Love of God
sermon.why: Preached by Keller at Redeemer Presbyterian Church from Psalm 107:31-43 as part of the series Four Ways to Live, Four Ways to Love, this sermon works directly from this psalm's closing call to consider the loving devotion of the LORD, drawing out how God's steadfast love, seen across the psalm's four rescue scenes, reorders how His people live and love in response.

quiz.q: Who is invited to speak in verse 2?

options.text: The redeemed of the LORD, those He has redeemed from the hand of the enemy.
options.note: The psalm's opening call is for those who have actually experienced God's rescue to publicly say so.

options.text: Only the priests serving in the temple.
options.note: The call is to the redeemed generally, not restricted to a priestly office.

options.text: The kings of the surrounding nations.
options.note: The psalm addresses Israel's own redeemed people, not foreign kings.

options.text: Only those who have never experienced trouble.
options.note: The whole psalm is about those who were in genuine distress before being redeemed, not those untouched by trouble.

quiz.q: What is the repeated four-part pattern in each of the psalm's rescue scenes?

options.text: Crisis, a cry to the LORD, rescue, and a call to give thanks.
options.note: This exact cycle repeats for wanderers, prisoners, the sick, and sailors alike, verses 4-9, 10-16, 17-22, and 23-32.

options.text: Crisis, self-rescue, pride, and silence.
options.note: The pattern is the opposite: each group is helpless and cries out to the LORD, who alone rescues them.

options.text: Warning, punishment, exile, and return.
options.note: This describes the broader history behind Book V, but the specific repeated pattern within each of the four scenes is crisis, cry, rescue, and thanks.

options.text: Prayer, fasting, sacrifice, and silence.
options.note: The specific repeated elements named in the text are crying out and giving thanks, not a fasting and sacrifice ritual in each scene.

quiz.q: What does God do for the sailors caught in the storm, verse 29?

options.text: He calms the storm to a whisper, and the waves of the sea are hushed.
options.note: This is the exact scene Jesus reenacts in Mark 4:35-41, commanding the wind and waves with the same authority.

options.text: He sends another ship to rescue them by force.
options.note: The text says God Himself calms the storm directly, not through a rescue vessel.

options.text: He teaches them better navigation skills.
options.note: Verse 27 says their own skill was useless; the rescue comes from God stilling the storm, not from improved sailing technique.

options.text: He allows the ship to sink but saves the sailors individually.
options.note: The text says the storm itself is calmed and the ship reaches its desired harbor, verse 30, not that the ship sinks.

quiz.q: According to verse 43, what should the wise person do?

options.text: Pay heed to these things and consider the loving devotion of the LORD.
options.note: The psalm closes by turning its four rescue stories into an invitation to reflect on and learn from God's steadfast love.

options.text: Avoid all risk and stay away from the sea, the desert, and prison.
options.note: The point is not risk-avoidance but recognizing and considering God's loving devotion in the midst of real distress.

options.text: Trust only in personal skill and preparation.
options.note: The psalm repeatedly shows human skill failing, verse 27, while God's rescue is what actually saves.

options.text: Forget the past and focus only on future plans.
options.note: The wise response named is to consider, remember, and reflect on God's loving devotion, not to forget the past.

quiz.q: How does this psalm connect most directly to the Gospels?

options.text: Jesus calms a storm with the same divine authority this psalm attributes to God alone, verse 29 and Mark 4:35-41.
options.note: The disciples' question, who is this that even wind and sea obey Him, is directly answered by this psalm's own claim about who commands the sea.

options.text: It has no connection to the Gospels.
options.note: Mark 4:35-41 deliberately echoes this exact scene and language.

options.text: It predicts the specific date of a future storm.
options.note: The psalm describes a pattern of rescue, not a prediction of a specific date or storm.

options.text: It is fulfilled only through advances in modern navigation.
options.note: The psalm's deepest fulfillment is Christ's authority over creation itself, not human technological progress.

questions: The psalm calls the redeemed to say so, verse 2, to publicly testify to God's rescue. What story of God's deliverance in your own life have you kept too private that needs to be spoken aloud?

Each of the four scenes follows the same pattern, crisis, cry, rescue, thanks. Which of the four pictures, wandering, imprisonment, sickness, or the storm, most describes where you are right now, and have you cried out to the LORD in it?
Verse 27 says the sailors' own skill was useless in the storm. Where are you relying on your own competence in a situation that actually calls for crying out to God instead?

Jesus calms the very kind of storm this psalm describes, revealing His divine authority, Mark 4:35-41. How does knowing that Christ Himself holds this same power over your storms change your prayers this week?

The psalm ends by calling the wise to consider the loving devotion of the LORD, verse 43. What would it look like this week to deliberately set aside time to consider, not just experience, God's steadfast love?

This psalm opens Book V by picking up the very refrain that closed Book IV. How does seeing your own life as one more chapter in this same, ongoing story of God's redeeming love shape your sense of belonging to His people?

translationNotes.note: From north and south renders a Hebrew phrase literally meaning from the north and the sea, an idiom for the western direction; either way the verse pictures the redeemed gathered from every point of the compass.
All their skill was useless can also be rendered all their wisdom was swallowed up or they were at their wits' end, each capturing the sailors' total helplessness apart from God's rescue.
The waves of the sea follows the Dead Sea Scrolls; the Masoretic Text reads their waves, a minor variant that does not affect the meaning, God silencing the storm completely.

Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Timothy Keller's 'Considering the Great Love of God' at gospelinlife.com, preached from Psalm 107:31-43.
